elements.text: Kinds of Testing or Sampling Under Phase II Environmental Hazard Assessments
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Examples of the kind of testing or sampling that occur under a Phase II assessment include but are not limited to the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: investigating the status of any enforcement actions related to neighboring properties under the Superfund or Resource, Conservation, and Recovery Acts;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: testing for underground storage leaks;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: sampling and analyzing the soil;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: sampling and analyzing the groundwater;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: testing soil or facilities that are suspected as being contaminated by polychlorinated biphenyls; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: sampling and analyzing bulk asbestos and developing related abatement and maintenance programs, if necessary.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B4-2.1-06, Remedial Actions for Environmental Hazard Assessments Below Standards (06/03/2020)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on remedial actions for environmental hazard assessments that are below standards, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Background
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Remedial Actions for Environmental Hazard Assessments Below Standards
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: 'No Further Action' Notices
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Background
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When an environmental hazard has been fully remediated, the hazard no longer poses any risks for future use of the land or structures or the need for ongoing activities to ensure human health and safety. Some hazards result in ongoing risks that require continual mitigation strategies to minimize potential harm. The environmental hazard is considered mitigated when those strategies sufficiently minimize the current and future risks to human health and safety.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the hazard will never be fully remediated but the impacts have been sufficiently mitigated to satisfy applicable regulatory standards, the site may be deemed suitable and safe for residential use. The lender has the delegation to review the environmental hazard and its mitigation plan to determine if the project meets Fannie Mae's requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Remedial Actions for Environmental Hazard Assessments Below Standards
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Properties that fail to meet a particular standard may be corrected through remedial actions and then retested. Remedial actions must be undertaken with the advice and written endorsement of a qualified environmental consultant. All remedial actions must be taken in accordance with all regulatory and good management standards.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Typically, lenders must confirm the completion and effectiveness of remedial actions based on the following conditions:
elements.label: text

elements.text: A qualified environmental consultant states in writing that remedial work needed to make the property eligible under the environmental standards can be completed within 90 days.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The project's developer or sponsor signs a contract with a qualified firm to perform the remedial work within 90 days.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The lender must warrant that the job has been satisfactorily completed and the property meets Fannie Mae's environmental eligibility standards.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the property is not remediated at the time of project approval, the project developer or sponsor must provide a performance escrow equal to 150% of the gross contract amount to ensure the completion of the remedial work. Loans securing units in the project cannot be sold to Fannie Mae before completion of the remediation.
elements.label: text

elements.text: 'No Further Action' Notices
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Some jurisdictions or government agencies will issue a 'no further action' notice (or letter) to alert the public that all available remediation steps for an environment hazard have been completed. A 'no further action' status for a specific environmental hazard may indicate that
elements.label: text

elements.text: the hazard has been fully remediated and the site is suitable for residential development,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: all applicable remediation actions have been taken but the site is not suitable for any type of development, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: other variations between these two opposing outcomes.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Due to the variation of what a 'no further action' status means, lenders cannot rely solely on that status to determine if the environmental hazard has been sufficiently resolved. For the project to meet Fannie Mae's requirements, lenders must determine if the specific 'no further action' condition has been sufficiently resolved.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lenders must follow Fannie Mae's appraisal and notification requirements for environmental hazards in B4-1.4-08, Environmental Hazards Appraisal Requirements, any time the subject property is impacted by an environmental hazard.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Limited Review Eligibility Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: In completing a Limited Review, the lender must ensure that the project and subject unit meet the eligibility requirements described in the following table.

elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The project meets Requirements Applicable to All Properties in a Condo, Co-op, or PUD Project described in  B4-2.1-01, General Information on Project Standards.

elements.label: text

elements.text: No more than 15% of the total number of units in the project are 60 days or more past due in the payment of each special assessment.
elements.label: text

elements.text: These requirements apply to both DU loan casefiles and manually underwritten loans.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the project and loan transaction are eligible for and meet all of the eligibility requirements of the Limited Review process, the lender is not required to validate that the project also meets the eligibility requirements of another project review type. However, if the LTV, CLTV, or HCLTV ratios exceed the limits above, or in the event the lender becomes aware of a circumstance that would cause the project or transaction to be ineligible under a Limited Review, the lender must use one of the other project review methods to determine project eligibility and the project must meet all of the eligibility requirements of that selected alternate project review type.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : For loans eligible for Limited Review that receive a CPM Approved by Fannie Mae message in DU, see CPM Approved by Fannie Mae Message in DU in B4-2.2-02, Full Review Process, for additional information.

elements.text: The Full Review process is a method for the review of new and established condo projects, co-op projects, and certain manufactured home projects. Lenders performing a Full Review must ensure that the project meets all applicable eligibility requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Unit and Project Types Eligible for Full Review
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A Full Review may be performed when the unit securing the loan is an attached unit located in one of the following project types:
elements.label: text

elements.text: an established condo project, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a new or newly converted condo project.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: A Full Review may also be performed when the property securing the loan is a manufactured home in an established condo project that is not subject to a community land trust, deed restriction, ground lease, or shared equity arrangement.
elements.label: text

elements.text: These projects may also be reviewed by Fannie Mae through the PERS process (see B4-2.2-06, Project Eligibility Review Service (PERS)).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Full Review requirements for units in co-op projects are addressed in  B4-2.3-02, Co-op Project Eligibility.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Condo Project Manager (CPM)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders must use CPM to assist in their Full Review of a condo project (except for projects containing manufactured homes). CPM is a Web-based tool designed to facilitate the lender's review of the project to determine if it meets Fannie Mae's project eligibility requirements. The lender must complete their own certification in CPM and document the loan file with the CPM decision by including the unexpired CPM Certification in the file.
elements.label: text

elements.text: CPM certifications are based solely on the data that the lender enters into CPM. The lender is responsible for reviewing the applicable project documentation to obtain the information needed to complete the project review and enter the data into CPM. The lender is also responsible for ensuring that all data entered into CPM is correct and that the project meets all applicable Fannie Mae eligibility requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: CPM is available on Fannie Mae's website.
elements.label: text

elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A loan submitted to DU will retain its Fannie Mae project approval status through the credit report expiration date specified on the DU Underwriting Findings report, provided it receives both:
elements.label: text

elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: an Approve/Eligible recommendation, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a message indicating the project has an Approved by Fannie Mae status in CPM.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: When any of the following are changed by the lender when the loan casefile is resubmitted to DU, the project eligibility status may change and result in the loss of the CPM Approved by Fannie Mae message:
elements.label: text

elements.text: CPM ID,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: project name,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: property address (state or zip code), or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: credit report.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: As with any other projects with an Approved by Fannie Mae status in CPM, lenders are required to validate compliance with all applicable insurance requirements outlined in Selling Guide Chapter B7-3, Property and Flood Insurance and Chapter B7-4, Liability and Fidelity/Crime Insurance Requirements . Also, see Additional Obligations of the Lender for Projects Approved by Fannie Mae below for additional requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : The loan is subject to delivery restrictions in CPM that may affect the loan's eligibility, even if the loan has received the CPM Approved by Fannie Mae message in DU.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Additional Obligations of the Lender for Projects Approved by Fannie Mae
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If a lender becomes aware of any information that could impact the eligibility status reflected in CPM (such as, significant deferred maintenance or major litigation), the lender must notify the CPM Management team (see E-1-02, List of Contacts) with the relevant data and information. Fannie Mae will evaluate the new information and its impact on eligibility. Before selling a loan secured by a unit in such a project, the lender must confirm that the project retains its approved status as of the note date, unless a loan has received and retains a CPM Approved by Fannie Mae message in DU. Notification must occur as soon as practicable but no later than five business days after becoming aware of such information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae reserves the right to change a project eligibility status designation if information acquired after approval or certification has an impact on a previously issued eligibility determination.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : Lenders are responsible for verifying and documenting that the project meets the applicable insurance requirements described in Chapters B7-3 and B7-4 . Fannie Mae does not review insurance policies as part of the review process, including when the loan has received a CPM Approved by Fannie Mae message in DU.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Full Review Eligibility Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When determining the eligibility of a condo project on the basis of a Full Review, lenders must ensure the condo project meets the eligibility requirements described in the following table.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Full Review Eligibility Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The project meets the Requirements Applicable to All Properties in a Condo, Co-op, or PUD Project described in B4-2.1-01, General Information on Project Standards.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The project must not be an ineligible project as described in  B4-2.1-03, Ineligible Projects.
elements.label: text

elements.text: No more than 15% of the total units in a project are 60 days or more past due on common expense assessments (also known as HOA fees). For example, a 100-unit project may not have more than 15 units that are 60 days or more past due.
elements.label: text

elements.text: This ratio is calculated by dividing the number of units with common expense assessments that are past due by 60 or more days by the total number of units in the project.
elements.label: text

elements.text: No more than 15% of the total units in a project are 60 days or more past due in the payment of each special assessment.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Full Review Eligibility Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders must review the HOA projected budget to determine that it
elements.label: text

elements.text: is adequate (that is, it includes allocations for line items pertinent to the type of condo project), and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: provides for the funding of replacement reserves for capital expenditures and deferred maintenance that is at least 10% of the budget.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: To determine whether the association has a minimum annual budgeted replacement reserve allocation of 10%, the lender must divide the annual budgeted replacement reserve allocation by the association's annual budgeted assessment income (which includes regular common expense fees).
elements.label: text

elements.text: The following types of income may be excluded from the reserve calculation:
elements.label: text

elements.text: incidental income on which the project does not rely for ongoing operations, maintenance, or capital improvements;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: income collected for utilities that would typically be paid by individual unit owners, such as cable TV or Internet access;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: income allocated to reserve accounts; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: special assessment income.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The lender may use a reserve study in lieu of calculating the replacement reserve of 10% provided the following conditions are met:
elements.label: text

elements.text: the lender obtains a copy of an acceptable reserve study and retains the study and the lender's analysis of the study in the project approval file,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the study demonstrates that the project has adequate funded reserves that provide financial protection for the project equivalent to Fannie Mae's standard reserve requirements,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the study demonstrates that the project's funded reserves meet or exceed the recommendations included in the reserve study, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the study meets Fannie Mae's requirements for replacement reserve studies listed at the end of this section.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: For projects in which the units are not separately metered for utilities, the lender must
elements.label: text

elements.text: determine that having multiple units on a single meter is common and customary in the local market where the project is located, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: confirm that the project budget includes adequate funding for utility payments.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The project must be located on contiguous parcels of land. It is acceptable for a project to be divided by public or private streets.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The structures within the project must be within a reasonable distance from each other.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Full Review Eligibility Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Common elements and facilities, such as recreational facilities and parking, must be consistent with the nature of the project and competitive in the marketplace.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Unit owners in the project must have the sole ownership interest in, and rights to the use of the project's facilities, common elements, and limited common elements, except as noted below.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Shared amenities are permitted only when two or more HOAs share amenities for the exclusive use of the unit owners. The associations must have an agreement in place governing the arrangement for shared amenities that includes the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: a description of the shared amenities subject to the arrangement;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a description of the terms under which unit owners in the project may use the shared amenities;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: provisions for the funding, management, and upkeep of the shared amenities; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: • provisions to resolve conflicts between the associations over the amenities.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Examples of shared amenities include, but are not limited to, clubhouses, recreational or fitness facilities, and swimming pools.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The developer may not retain any ownership interest in any of the facilities related to the project. The amenities and facilities-including parking and recreational facilities-may not be subject to a lease between the unit owners or the HOA and another party. Parking amenities provided under commercial leases or parking permit arrangements with parties unrelated to the developer are acceptable.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae permits the financing of a single or multiple parking space(s) with the mortgage provided that the parking space(s) and subject unit are included on one deed as evidenced on the legal description in the mortgage. In such cases, the LTV, CLTV, and HCLTV ratios are based on the combined value of the residential unit and the parking space(s).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Phase I and II environmental hazard assessments are not required for condo projects unless the lender identifies an environmental problem through the performance of its project underwriting or due diligence.
elements.label: text

elements.text: In the event that environmental problems are identified, the problems must be acceptable, as described in E-2-02, Suggested Format for Phase I Environmental Hazard Assessments.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Full Review Eligibility Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For investment property transactions in established projects at least 50% of the total units in the project must be conveyed to principal residence or second home purchasers. This requirement does not apply if the subject mortgage is for a principal residence or second home.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Financial institution-owned REO units that are for sale (not rented) are considered owner- occupied when calculating the 50% owner-occupancy ratio requirement.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the project was a gut rehabilitation project, all rehabilitation work involved in a condo conversion must have been completed in a professional manner.
elements.label: text

elements.text: 'Gut rehabilitation' refers to the renovation of a property down to the shell of the structure, including the replacement of all HVAC and electrical components (unless the HVAC and electrical components are up to current code).
elements.label: text

elements.text: For a conversion that was legally created during the past three years, the architect's or engineer's report (or functional equivalent), that was originally obtained for the conversion must comment favorably on the structural integrity of the project and the condition and remaining useful life of the major project components, such as the heating and cooling systems, plumbing, electrical systems, elevators, boilers, roof, etc.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For additional information applicable to condo projects subject to a ground lease, see B2-3-03, Special Property Eligibility and Underwriting Considerations: Leasehold Estates.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : If the project is a newly converted non-gut rehabilitation project with more than four residential units, lenders must submit the project to Fannie Mae for review and approval. See B4-2.2-06, Project Eligibility Review Service (PERS), for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Additional Requirements - For Condo Projects Consisting of Manufactured Homes
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When determining the eligibility of condo project consisting of manufactured homes on the basis of a Full Review, lenders must ensure the property and project meet the eligibility requirements described in the following table.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Additional Requirements for Condo Projects Consisting of Manufactured Home Units
elements.label: text

elements.text: As described in  B4-2.2-06, Project Eligibility Review Service (PERS), certain manufactured home projects must be submitted to PERS. Lenders must perform a pre-PERS submission review to confirm the project meets the Full Review and other requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Additional Requirements for Condo Projects Consisting of Manufactured Home Units
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The condo project must meet all Full Review requirements, as applicable.
elements.label: text

elements.text: CPM should not be used to complete the Full Review because it does not contain all the
elements.label: text

elements.text: requirements that apply to condo projects consisting of manufactured homes.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The project must not contain campgrounds or other facilities for transient or mobile units.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The project legal documents must require a provision for land-lease 'hold-out' units to be converted into the condo structure upon transfer, sale, or refinance of property. Land lease 'hold-out' units are limited to 25% or less of the total units in the project.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Land-lease hold-out units are units where the structure is owned by an individual, but the land
elements.label: text

elements.text: is leased from the HOA or project sponsor. These units were not converted to condo ownership
elements.label: text

elements.text: when the project converted to a condo regime.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Replacement Reserve Studies
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Reserve studies may be used to determine the appropriate level of reserves the HOA must maintain to ensure the project's long-term success. Reserve studies will also provide useful information regarding the adequacy of the HOA's current reserve funds and offer recommendations to meet funding goals in the event the HOA has under-reserved for its needs in the past. The lender may review the most current reserve study or a reserve study update provided it has been completed within three years of the date on which the lender approves the project.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Reserve studies must be prepared by an independent third party that has specific expertise in completing reserve studies. This expertise may include any of the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: a reserve study professional with reserve study credentials,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a construction engineer,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a certified public accountant who specializes in reserve studies, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: any professional with demonstrated knowledge of and experience in completing reserve studies.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: While Fannie Mae does not require that a standard format be used for the reserve study, the following items must be addressed:
elements.label: text

elements.text: all major components and elements of the project's common areas for which repair, maintenance, or replacement is expected;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the condition and remaining useful life of each major component;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: an estimate of the cost of repair, replacement, restoration, or maintenance of major components;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: an estimate of the total annual contributions required to defray costs (minus the existing reserves funded for this purpose), including inflation;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: an analysis of existing funded reserves; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a suggested reserve funding plan.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Note : Individual states may have various statutes concerning the use and content of reserve studies.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Fannie Mae requires that a reserve study used by the lender in its analysis meet or exceed requirements set forth in relevant state statutes.
elements.label: text

elements.text: B4-2.2-03, Full Review: Additional Eligibility Requirements for Units in New and Newly Converted Condo Projects (06/05/2018)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on the Full Review of units in new and newly converted condo projects, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Additional Requirements for Units in New and Newly Converted Condo Projects
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Condo Project Legal Document Review Requirements for Units in New or Newly Converted Projects
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Additional Requirements for Units in New and Newly Converted Condo Projects
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When performing a Full Review of new or newly converted condo projects, lenders must ensure compliance with the following additional requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: Projects consisting of units in new or newly converted projects in Florida must be reviewed by Fannie Mae through the PERS process. See B4-2.2-04, Geographic-Specific Condo Project Considerations.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Full Review Requirements - For Units in New or Newly Converted Condo Projects
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The project, or the subject legal phase, must be 'substantially complete' unless other completion arrangements have been approved by Fannie Mae through the PERS review process.
elements.label: text

elements.text: There may not be more than one legal phase per building.
elements.label: text

elements.text: 'Substantially complete' means that
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a certificate of occupancy or other substantially similar document has been issued by the applicable governmental agency for the project or subject phase; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: all the units and buildings in the legal phase in which the unit securing the mortgage is located are complete, subject to the installation of buyer selection items, such as appliances.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Note: Fannie Mae does not require the installation of typical buyer selection items such as appliances, floor coverings, counter tops, or light fixtures that are common and customary for the market, although buyer selections that involve the modification of a unit floor plan must be complete. Lenders are expected to obtain appropriate documentation to verify that all buyer selection items for the unit being financed are properly installed prior to closing.
elements.label: text

elements.text: At least 50% of the total units in the project or subject legal phase must have been conveyed or be under contract for sale to principal residence or second home purchasers.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For a specific legal phase or phases in a new project, at least 50% of the total units in the subject legal phase(s), considered together with all prior legal phases, must have been conveyed or be under contract for sale to principal residence or second home purchasers.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: For the purposes of this review process, a project consisting of one building cannot have more than one legal phase.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Individual units in new condo projects must be available for immediate occupancy at the time of loan closing.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the project is part of a larger development, and the unit owners are required to pay monthly assessments of more than $50 to a separate master association for that development, lenders must review the overall development plan for the master association to evaluate the acceptability of the project.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The overall development plan of the project must be reviewed and the following must be acceptable:
elements.label: text

elements.text: consistency of future and existing improvements,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: time limitations for expansion, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: reciprocal easements between legal phases.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Full Review Requirements - For Units in New or Newly Converted Condo Projects
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For projects (or the subject legal phase) that are only substantially complete rather than 100% complete, lenders must determine that acceptable completion assurance arrangements that guarantee the future completion of all project facilities, common elements, and limited common elements have been provided. These assurance arrangements may include
elements.label: text

elements.text: cash deposits,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: letters of credit,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: assignments of certificates of deposit, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: assignments of other assets that can be easily converted to cash.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Similar arrangements must be provided to support assurances against construction and structural defects. The assurances must
elements.label: text

elements.text: protect each unit against defects that become apparent within one year from the date of its settlement, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: cover all common facilities for one year from the date on which units that represent at least 60% of the votes in the HOA have been transferred.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The developer or sponsor should provide for and promote the unit owners' early participation in the management of the project.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The project must meet the condo project legal document requirements in the following section.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Condo Project Legal Document Review Requirements for Units in New or Newly Converted Projects
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The table below provides Fannie Mae's requirements for the review of the condo project's legal documents for units in new and newly converted condo projects containing more than four residential units.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Condo Project Legal Document Review Requirements - For Units in New or Newly Converted Condo Projects
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Limitations on Ability to Sell/Right of First Refusal
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Any right of first refusal in the condo project documents will not adversely impact the rights of a mortgagee or its assignee to:
elements.label: text

elements.text: foreclose or take title to a condo unit pursuant to the remedies in the mortgage,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: accept a deed or assignment in lieu of foreclosure in the event of default by a mortgagor, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: sell or lease a unit acquired by the mortgagee or its assignee.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Condo Project Legal Document Review Requirements - For Units in New or Newly Converted Condo Projects
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Rights of Condo Mortgagees and Guarantors
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The project documents must give the mortgagee and guarantor of the mortgage on any unit in a condo project the right to timely written notice of:
elements.label: text

elements.text: any condemnation or casualty loss that affects either a material portion of the project or the unit securing its mortgage;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: • any 60-day delinquency in the payment of assessments or
elements.label: text

elements.text: charges owed by the owner of any unit on which it holds the
elements.label: text

elements.text: mortgage;
elements.label: text

elements.text: a lapse, cancellation, or material modification of any insurance policy maintained by the homeowners' association; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: any proposed action that requires the consent of a specified percentage of mortgagees.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: First Mortgagee's Rights Confirmed
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: No provision of the condo project documents gives a condo unit owner or any other party priority over any rights of the first mortgagee of the condo unit pursuant to its mortgage in the case of payment to the unit owner of insurance proceeds or condemnation awards for losses to or a taking of condo units and/or common elements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Condo Project Legal Document Review Requirements - For Units in New or Newly Converted Condo Projects
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Amendments to Documents
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Required provisions related to amendments to project documents are as follow:
elements.label: text

elements.text: The project documents must provide that amendments of a material adverse nature to mortgagees be agreed to by mortgagees that represent at least 51% of the votes of unit estates that are subject to mortgages.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The project documents must provide for any action to terminate the legal status of the project after substantial destruction or condemnation occurs or for other reasons to be agreed to by mortgagees that represent at least 51% of the votes of the unit estates that are subject to mortgages.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The project documents may provide for implied approval to be assumed when a mortgagee fails to submit a response to any written proposal for an amendment within 60 days after it receives proper notice of the proposal, provided the notice was delivered by certified or registered mail, with a return receipt requested. Notwithstanding the foregoing, project documents that were recorded prior to August 23, 2007, may provide for implied approval to be assumed when a mortgagee fails to submit a response to any written proposal for an amendment within 30 days after it receives proper notice of the proposal, provided the notice was delivered by certified or registered mail, with a return receipt requested.
elements.label: list_item

elements.label: text

elements.text: There are no recently issued Announcements related to this topic.

elements.text: B4-2.2-05, FHA-Approved Condo Review Eligibility (09/04/2018)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on FHA-approved condo review eligibility, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Project Requirements
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Document Retention
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae accepts delivery of FHA mortgage loans in FHA-approved condo projects that appear on the FHAapproved condo list. For conventional mortgage loans, Fannie Mae will accept delivery of mortgages in established projects on the FHA-approved list provided the approval was completed by FHA HUD Review and Approval Process (HRAP) rather than through an FHA Direct Endorsement Lender Review and Approval Process (DELRAP). FHA condo project approval is not acceptable for conventional mortgage loans secured by units in new or newly converted condo projects.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lenders may search for FHA-approved condo projects by location, name, or project status online at HUD.gov or through CPM.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Lenders must maintain copies of the FHA approval documentation in the loan file.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Project Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For FHA mortgage loans, lenders must ensure that
elements.label: text

elements.text: the FHA standard conditions have been met for presale, occupancy status, and completion;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: any additional conditions noted by FHA have been met;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the project is not an ineligible project as defined in B4-2.1-03, Ineligible Projects;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the project is covered by the required insurance as set forth in Subpart B7-4, Liability and Fidelity/Crime Insurance Requirements for Project Developments; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the project is not comprised of manufactured homes.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: For conventional mortgage loans, lenders must ensure that
elements.label: text

elements.text: the project meets Fannie Mae's criteria to be considered an established project;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the project is not comprised of manufactured homes;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the project meets the Requirements Applicable to All Properties in a Condo, Co-op, or PUD Project described in B4-2.1-01, General Information on Project Standards;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the project is not an ineligible project as described in B4-2.1-03, Ineligible Projects; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: any additional conditions noted by FHA have been met.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Document Retention
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When lenders deliver mortgage loans secured by condo units in an FHA-approved project, lenders must retain the documentation as set forth in Document Retention in B4-2.1-01, General Information on Project Standards.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: PERS is a review method available to lenders to submit new, newly converted, and established projects to Fannie Mae to determine eligibility. Some projects must be submitted to PERS while a PERS submission is optional for other projects, as shown in the table below.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Standard PERS Process
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Streamlined PERS Process
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Required for:
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: new condo projects consisting of manufactured homes (including those subject to a community land trust, deed restrictions, ground lease, or shared equity arrangement);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: new PUD projects consisting of manufactured homes subject to a community land trust, deed restriction, ground lease, or shared equity arrangement;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Additional Requirements-For Newly Converted Non-Gut Rehabilitation Condo or Co-op Projects
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A non-gut rehabilitation refers to the renovation of a property that does not involve structural or functional changes, such as the replacement of all HVAC and electrical components. Rather, the rehabilitation might include, for example, the replacement of appliances and carpeting.
elements.label: text

elements.text: In order for a newly converted non-gut rehabilitation condo or co-op project to receive project approval through the standard PERS process, the project must comply with the following requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lender Pre-PERS Submission Review Requirements - For Newly Converted Non-Gut Rehabilitation Condo or Co-op Projects
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The project cannot be an ineligible project in accordance with B4-2.1-03, Ineligible Projects.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Lender Pre-PERS Submission Review Requirements - For Newly Converted Non-Gut Rehabilitation Condo or Co-op Projects
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For condo projects-The condo project must comply with all requirements of the Full Review (as provided in B4-2.2-02, Full Review Process and B4-2.2-03, Full Review: Additional Eligibility Requirements for Units in New and Newly Converted Condo Projects).
elements.label: text

elements.text: For co-op projects-The co-op project must comply with all requirements for co-op projects (as provided in  B4-2.3-02, Co-op Project Eligibility, and B4-2.3-03, Legal Requirements for Co-op Projects).
elements.label: text

elements.text: All rehabilitation work involved in the condo or co-op conversion must have been completed in a professional manner.
elements.label: text

elements.text: A current reserve study prepared by a qualified, independent professional company, accompanied by an engineer's report, or functional equivalent, must comment favorably on the structural integrity of the project and the remaining useful life of the major project components.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The project budget must contain line items for
elements.label: text

elements.text: • reserves to adequately support the costs identified in the reserve study, and
elements.label: text

elements.text: a utility contingency of at least 10% of the previous year's utility costs if the utilities are not separately metered.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Funds to cover the total cost of any items identified in the reserve study or engineer's report that need to be replaced within five years from the date of the study must be deposited in the reserve account of the HOA or of the co-op corporation, in addition to the amount stated immediately above.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The developer must provide a detailed description of the work proposed or already completed in order for the project units to be ready for sale.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Generally, at least 50% of the total condo units or co-op stocks or shares in the project or subject legal phase must have been conveyed or be under contract for purchase to principal residence or second home purchasers.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Up to 30% of the units (or of stocks or shares for co-ops) in projects that are subject to rent regulations, which protect tenants from eviction (if they have chosen not to purchase their unit), will be permitted.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Phasing of projects (single building or multiple buildings) will be considered on a project basis.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.label: text

elements.text: Rehabilitation Condo or Co-op Projects
elements.label: text

elements.text: The project sponsor or developer must provide a comprehensive sales and marketing strategy.
elements.label: text

elements.text: All projects are subject to a site inspection.

elements.text: PUD Project Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For a project to qualify as a PUD, all of the following requirements must be met:
elements.label: text

elements.text: each unit owner's membership in the HOA must be automatic and nonseverable,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the payment of assessments related to the unit must be mandatory,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: common property and improvements must be owned and maintained by an HOA for the benefit and use of the unit owners, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the subject unit must not be legally created as part of a condo or co-op project.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Zoning is not a basis for classifying a project or subdivision as a PUD. Units in projects or subdivisions simply zoned as PUDs that include the following characteristics are not defined as PUD projects under Fannie Mae's policies. These projects
elements.label: text

elements.text: have no common property and improvements,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: do not require the establishment of and membership in an HOA, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: do not require the payment of assessments.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Fannie Mae classifies PUD projects as either
elements.label: text

elements.text: Type E-established PUD projects in which the developer has turned over voting control of the HOA to the unit purchasers.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Type F-new PUD projects in which the developer has not turned over voting control of the HOA to the unit purchasers.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Certain PUD projects require submission to PERS. See  B4-2.2-06, Project Eligibility Review Service (PERS) for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: New or established PUD projects consisting of single-width and/or multi-width manufactured homes not subject to a community land trust, deed restriction, ground lease, or shared equity arrangement may be reviewed in accordance with B4-2.1-02, Waiver of Project Review.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Eligibility Requirements for Units in PUD Projects
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders must determine that the PUD project and subject unit meet the requirements described in Requirements Applicable to All Properties in a Condo, Co-op, or PUD Project in B4-2.1-01, General Information on Project Standards.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : Any unit legally created as part of a condo or co-op project located within a larger PUD project or master association must meet the applicable requirements for condo or co-op projects.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For additional information applicable to PUD projects subject to a ground lease, see B2-3-03, Special Property Eligibility and Underwriting Considerations: Leasehold Estates.

elements.text: Co-op Project Eligibility Overview
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae purchases or securitizes co-op share loans for units in co-op projects from lenders specially approved to sell such loans to Fannie Mae. Lenders must determine the acceptability of a co-op project, unless the project is a newly converted non-gut rehabilitation of a co-op project. Such projects must be submitted via the Project Eligibility Review Service (PERS) to Fannie Mae for review.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lack of available co-op project data and the inconsistent reporting of co-op project information can be a barrier to obtaining affordable financing for co-op housing. Lenders are responsible for determining the most appropriate method for obtaining information about co-op projects and the accuracy of the information they obtain.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For additional information, see:
elements.label: text

elements.text: A2-1-01, Contractual Obligations for Sellers/Servicers,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B4-2.3-04, Loan Eligibility for Co-op Share Loans,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B4-2.2-06, Project Eligibility Review Service (PERS),
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: B4-2.1-01, General Information on Project Standards.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Request for Co-op Project Information
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The Request for Cooperative Project Information ( Form 1074) includes the project information that lenders, investors, and mortgage insurers may use in their evaluation of the eligibility of a co-op project, and provides an efficient means of collecting basic project information from co-op project management agents, boards of directors, or sponsors/developers.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Eligibility Requirements for Co-op Projects
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The table below provides project eligibility requirements for co-op projects.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Full Review Eligibility Requirements - For New and Established Co-op Projects
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For a co-op share loan to be eligible for sale, the co-op project in which the secured unit is located must qualify as a cooperative housing corporation under Section 216 of the Internal Revenue Service Code. The lender's loan or project approval file must contain evidence regarding the project's compliance with Section 216.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : If the co-op project does not meet Section 216 requirements, Fannie Mae will not purchase a co-op share loan from within the project.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The co-op housing project must
elements.label: text

elements.text: be designed principally for residential use;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: consist of two or more units; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: be located in an area that has a demonstrated market acceptance for the co-op form of ownership, as reflected by the availability of similar comparable sales for co-op units in the market area.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The project may be owned in fee simple or be a leasehold estate (subject to a ground lease).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note
elements.label: text

elements.text: : For additional information applicable to co-op projects subject to a ground lease, see
elements.label: text

elements.text: B2-3-03, Special Property Eligibility and Underwriting Considerations: Leasehold Estates
elements.label: text

elements.text: B2-3-03, Special Property Eligibility and Underwriting Considerations: Leasehold Estates
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Full Review Eligibility Requirements - For New and Established Co-op Projects
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender is responsible for determining that the co-op cooperation holds title to the property of the co-op project, including the dwelling units. A type of co-op project that does not meet these requirements is one in which the borrower, not the co-op corporation, owns their dwelling unit in the project. Co-op share loans in these projects are commonly referred to as 'land-home' or 'land-lease' co-op projects and require special approval for delivery to Fannie Mae.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The co-op corporation must have good and marketable title to the property, including the dwelling units and amenities. The project premises must be free and clear of liens and encumbrances in accordance with B7-2-05, Title Exceptions and Impediments.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The blanket project mortgage may be a market-rate FHA-insured mortgage or a conventional mortgage.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The blanket mortgage for the project may be a balloon mortgage. The remaining term may not be less than three months, provided refinance terms have been secured, or the loan will be paid off before maturity. If the balloon mortgage incorporates an adjustable-rate feature, and the remaining term is less than three years but not less than six months, the current interest rate may not be subject to an interest rate adjustment prior to the maturity date.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae purchases or securitizes co-op share loans regardless of whether Fannie Mae owns the blanket mortgage. However, if Fannie Mae owns an interest in the blanket co-op project mortgage, the maximum mortgage amount available to the borrower must be reduced by the portion of the unpaid principal balance of the blanket mortgage(s) that is attributable to the subject unit's ownership interest.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae will not purchase or securitize co-op share loans if the co-op project is an ineligible project type, regardless of the characteristics of the share loan. See B4-2.1-03, Ineligible Projects.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The project must not be a manufactured housing project.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The project must meet Fannie Mae's insurance requirements, as stated in Subpart B7, Insurance .
elements.label: text

elements.text: Co-op projects may be newly constructed or conversions of existing buildings.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Full Review Eligibility Requirements - For New and Established Co-op Projects
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: All newly converted non-gut rehabilitation of co-op share projects must be approved through the PERS process.
elements.label: text

elements.text: A newly converted non-gut rehabilitation co-op project is defined as follows:
elements.label: text

elements.text: a project for which the building has been recently converted from another use such as, but not limited to, apartment use, hotel building, or warehouse;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the renovation work did not involve structural or functional changes, such as the replacement of all HVAC and electrical components and was limited to cosmetic or design changes such as painting, flooring, and appliances; and,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the project meets the criteria for being a new project because any of the following conditions exist with respect to the status of the project:
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ⚬ fewer than 90% of the stock or shares have been sold to purchasers;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ⚬ the developer or sponsor is in control of the co-op corporation;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ⚬ the project is not fully completed, such as proposed construction, new construction, or the proposed or incomplete conversion of an existing building to a co-op; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ⚬ the project is subject to additional phasing or annexation.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The following newly converted projects may be reviewed by the lender through the standard co-op review process rather than being submitted to PERS:
elements.label: text

elements.text: any non-gut rehabilitation conversion project that was converted at least three years prior to the co-op share loan note date that is considered 'newly converted' solely because more than 20% of the stock or shares are owned by the sponsor as described in the single entity ownership provisions in B4-2.3-05, Geographic-Specific Co-op Project Considerations; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: two- to four-unit non-gut rehabilitation conversions.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: All units, common areas, and facilities within the project must be 100% complete. The project cannot be subject to additional phasing or annexation. All construction and rehabilitation for the project must be completed in a professional manner before Fannie Mae purchases or securitizes the share loan, unless the Project Standards Team approves delivery at an earlier date.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Phase I and II environmental hazard assessments are not required for co-op projects unless the lender identifies an environmental problem through the performance of its project underwriting and due diligence.
elements.label: text

elements.text: In the event that environmental problems are identified, the problems must be determined to be acceptable, as described in E-2-02, Suggested Format for Phase I Environmental Hazard Assessments.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Full Review Eligibility Requirements - For New and Established Co-op Projects
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Stock, share, or other contractual agreement evidencing ownership, and the accompanying occupancy rights that represent at least 50% of the total number of stock or shares in the co- op corporation and the related occupancy rights of units in the project must have been sold and conveyed (or, for new construction, must be under contract for sale) to principal residence purchasers.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The project's most recent operating budget and most recent two years audited financial statements, or corporate tax returns must
elements.label: text

elements.text: • be consistent with the nature of the project,
elements.label: text

elements.text: • provide for adequate cash flow to service the current debt and operating expenses, and
elements.label: text

elements.text: • provide for adequate replacement and operating reserves.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the most recent budget is not available, the lender may rely on a review of the co-op corporation's two most recent years audited financial statements or corporate tax returns to determine that the financial requirements in this section have been met.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If there is any negative cash flow for the present year, it may not exceed 5% of the co-op project's most recent audited financial statement and may not have two consecutive years' operating loss. The negative cash flow must be attributable to an isolated non-recurring expense.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If negative cash flow is from sponsor-held unsold units, the following requirements must be met:
elements.label: text

elements.text: such negative cash flow (including, but not limited to, any principal and interest payments relating to the financing obtained by the sponsor to acquire the co-op project) will not exceed an amount equal to 5% of the project's annual operating budget;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: if the sponsor fails to pay the monthly assessments relating to all co-op units owned by the sponsor, the monthly assessments of the co-op share owners other than the sponsor will not increase by more than 10%;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the sponsor is current on all financial obligations relating to any other project in which the sponsor owns or holds more than 10% of the units; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the sponsor has not pledged any of the shares of the co-op project as security for any loan other than to secure, in whole or in part, the financing obtained by the sponsor to acquire the co-op project.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The project must have a good financial record, with no more than 15% of the owners being more than 60 days past due in the payment of their financial obligations to the co-op corporation. Note: This includes payment of each special assessment.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Full Review Eligibility Requirements - For New and Established Co-op Projects
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If the project is a recipient of subsidies or similar benefits (such as tax or assessment abatements) that will terminate partially or fully within the next three years, the lender must evaluate the impact the expiration of such benefit will have on the project. If the benefit is scheduled to expire within three years from the note date, the lender must include the higher monthly fees in the borrower's monthly liabilities for debt-to-income ratio qualifying purposes.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The project and share loan documentation must comply with any specific legal requirements established for the state in which the project is located.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The co-op corporation must have the sole ownership interest in the project's facilities, common elements, and limited common elements, except as noted below.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Shared amenities are permitted only when two or more residential projects share amenities for the exclusive use of the unit owners. The associations or corporations must have an agreement in place governing the arrangement for shared amenities that includes the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: a description of the shared amenities subject to the arrangement;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a description of the terms under which unit owners in the project may use the shared amenities;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: provisions for the funding, management, and upkeep of the shared amenities; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: • provisions to resolve conflicts between the residential projects regarding the amenities.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Examples of shared amenities include, but are not limited to, clubhouses, recreational or fitness facilities, and swimming pools.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The developer may not retain any ownership interest in any of the facilities related to the project. The amenities and facilities, including parking and recreational facilities, may not be subject to a lease between the unit owners or the co-op corporation and another party. Parking amenities provided under commercial leases or parking permit arrangements with parties unrelated to the developer are acceptable.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Eligibility Requirements for Condop Projects
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A condop is a mixed-use development that is a condo project or two or more condo units in which one condo unit is an exclusively residential co-op project and the remaining unit(s) are used exclusively for commercial purposes. The land may be held in fee simple or under a ground lease by the condo project. Condop project refers to the co-op project that operates inside a condop development.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The table below provides project eligibility requirements for condop projects.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Full Review Eligibility Requirements For Condop Projects
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The condop project must comply with all other requirements for co-op projects including those provided in:
elements.label: text

elements.text: B4-2.3-02, Co-op Project Eligibility,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B4-2.3-03, Legal Requirements for Co-op Projects, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B4-2.3-04, Loan Eligibility for Co-op Share Loans.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: In addition to all other requirements for co-op projects, the condop project must account for the majority of the condo unit holders' square footage of non-common element space in the condo. A loan secured by a unit in a condop project must meet all other requirements for coop loans.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The co-op project must hold the majority of the undivided percent interest in the common elements. The common elements include interest in the land, foundations, and shared building systems and spaces that serve more than one dwelling or commercial unit, including parking.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The project board of directors must exercise effective control of the condo board, including voting rights proportional to their common interest in the condo project.  However, the commercial condo unit owners may have other subordinate governance mechanisms relating exclusively to the use and operation of the commercial condo units.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The current operating budget and most recent two years audited financial statements or corporate tax returns for the co-op and the condo projects must show that each entity:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Generates revenue greater than expenses, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Maintains sufficient liquid assets for unexpected events.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the most recent budget is not available, the lender may rely on a review of the co-op corporation's two most recent years audited financial statements or corporate tax returns to determine that the financial requirements in this section have been met.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Any loans between the condo and co-op projects must be in good standing.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The co-op and condo components of the condop development must be evaluated together to ensure overall financial and operational stability.
elements.label: text
